message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  In order to quantitatively characterize the epidemic of COVID-19, useful relations among parameters describing an epidemic in general are derived based on the Kermack–McKendrick model. The first relation is
                  <jats:bold>1/</jats:bold>
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:bold>
                      <jats:italic>grow</jats:italic>
                    </jats:bold>
                  </jats:sub>
                  =
                  <jats:bold>1/</jats:bold>
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:bold>
                      <jats:italic>trans</jats:italic>
                    </jats:bold>
                  </jats:sub>
                  −
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>1/</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:bold>
                      <jats:italic>inf</jats:italic>
                    </jats:bold>
                  </jats:sub>
                  , where
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>grow</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  is the time constant of the exponential growth of an epidemic,
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>trans</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  is the time for a pathogen to be transmitted from one patient to uninfected person, and the infectious time
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>inf</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  is the time during which the pathogen keeps its power of transmission. The second relation
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>p(</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  ∞
                  <jats:bold>)</jats:bold>
                  ≈
                  <jats:bold>1</jats:bold>
                  −
                  <jats:bold>exp(</jats:bold>
                  −
                  <jats:bold>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:bold>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:bold>
                  </jats:sub>
                  −
                  <jats:bold>1)/0</jats:bold>
                  .
                  <jats:bold>60)</jats:bold>
                  is the relation between
                  <jats:italic>p(</jats:italic>
                  ∞), the final size of the disaster defined by the ratio of the total infected people to the population of the society, and the basic reproduction number,
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  , which is the number of persons infected by the transmission of the pathogen from one infected person during the infectious time. The third relation
                  <jats:bold>1/</jats:bold>
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:bold>
                      <jats:italic>end</jats:italic>
                    </jats:bold>
                  </jats:sub>
                  =
                  <jats:bold>1/</jats:bold>
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:bold>
                      <jats:italic>inf</jats:italic>
                    </jats:bold>
                  </jats:sub>
                  −
                  <jats:bold>(1</jats:bold>
                  −
                  <jats:bold>
                    <jats:italic>p(</jats:italic>
                  </jats:bold>
                  ∞
                  <jats:bold>))/</jats:bold>
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:bold>
                      <jats:italic>trans</jats:italic>
                    </jats:bold>
                  </jats:sub>
                  gives the decay time constant
                  <jats:italic>
                    <jats:bold>τ</jats:bold>
                  </jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>end</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  at the ending stage of the epidemic. Derived relations are applied to influenza in Japan in 2019 for characterizing the epidemic.
                </jats:p>
title: Relations of parameters for describing the epidemic of COVID―19 by the Kermack―McKendrick model

reference.unstructured: Toshihisa Tomie , “Understanding the present status and forecasting of COVID―19 in Wuhan”, medRxiv preprint https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.02.13.20022251
W.O. Kermack and A.G. McKendrick , “A contribution to the mathematical theory of epidemics”, Proc. R. Soc. London A 115 (1927) 700.
National Health Commission of the People’s Republic of China, Prevention of Epidemics (in Chinese) http://www.nhc.gov.cn/xcs/yqtb/list_gzbd.shtml
Health Commission of Hubei Province, A nti-Prevention New Coronary Disease Infection, Information (in Chinese) http://wjw.hubei.gov.cn/bmdt/ztzl/fkxxgzbdgrfyyq/xxfb/index.shtml

National Institute of Infectious Diseases, IDWR Bulletin Data 4th Week 2020 https://www.niid.go.jp/niid/ja/data.html “Number of reports at fixed disease points-Comparison with the past 10 years- “(in Japanese)

National Institute of Infectious Diseases and Ministry of Health, Labor and Welfare Tuberculosis Infectious Disease Division, Page 7 in “Influenza in this year 2018/2019” (in Japanese) https://www.niid.go.jp/niid/images/idsc/disease/influ/fludoco1819.pdf
